notices.title: industrial visit
notices.content: An industrial visit bridges the gap between classroom theory and practical application. It exposes students to real-world operations, corporate culture, and modern technologies. Core content involves three main phases: Pre-visit Objectives, On-site Observations, and Post-visit Reporting.

notes.title: Data Structures - UNIT 1: Linear Lists & Linked Lists
notes.subject: Data Structures

notes.title: Data Structures - UNIT 2: Stacks, Queues, & Heaps
notes.subject: Data Structures
notes.content:
--- UNIT-II SUMMARY ---

1. Stack ADT:
- LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) linear structure.
- Basic Operations:
  - push(item): Inserts item on top. Check for overflow: if(top == max - 1).
  - pop(): Removes top item. Check for underflow: if(top == -1).
- Applications:
  - Infix to Postfix/Prefix expression conversion & evaluation.
  - Tower of Hanoi problem solving.
  - Recursive function calls & compile-time call stacks.

2. Queue ADT:
- FIFO (First-In-First-Out) linear structure.
- Insertions at 'rear', deletions at 'front'.
- Types of Queues:
  - Simple Queue (using array/linked list). Underflow if front == -1 or front > rear; Overflow if rear == max - 1.
  - Circular Queue: Resolves wasted space of linear queues. Array indices are treated as circular using modulo division: rear = (rear + 1) % max.

3. Priority Queue & Heaps:
- Collection of elements with associated priority.
- Elements are removed in order of priority (Min Priority Queue or Max Priority Queue).
- Heap Structure:
  - Max Heap: A complete binary tree where the value of each node is >= values of its children.
  - Min Heap: A complete binary tree where the value of each node is <= values of its children.
  - Height of heap is O(log n), making heap insertion & deletion extremely efficient at O(log n) complexity.

notes.title: Data Structures - UNIT 3: Searching, Sorting, & Graphs
notes.subject: Data Structures
notes.content:
--- UNIT-III SUMMARY ---

1. Searching Methods:
- Linear Search: Scans sequentially. Time complexity: O(n) worst/average, O(1) best.
- Binary Search: Divide-and-conquer on sorted arrays. Time complexity: O(log n) worst/average, O(1) best.

2. Sorting Algorithms & Time Complexities:
- Bubble Sort: O(n^2) worst/average, O(n) best-case (optimized).
- Selection Sort: O(n^2) worst/average/best (always scans rest of array).
- Insertion Sort: O(n^2) worst/average, O(n) best (nearly-sorted data).
- Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst-case (unbalanced pivot). Divide-and-conquer using pivot partitioning.
- Merge Sort: O(n log n) worst/average/best. Stable sorting requiring O(n) extra space.
- Heap Sort: O(n log n) worst/average/best. In-place sorting using binary heap heapify structure.

3. Graphs:
- Set of vertices V(G) and edges E(G).
- Graph Representations:
  - Adjacency Matrix: 2D array of size V x V where matrix[i][j] = 1 if edge exists, else 0.
  - Adjacency List: Array of linked lists representing adjacent vertices.
  - Incidence Matrix: Matrix of size V x E.
- Traversals:
  - Depth First Search (DFS): Backtracking recursive or iterative stack traversal.
  - Breadth First Search (BFS): Layer-by-layer queue traversal.

notes.title: Data Structures - UNIT 4: Dictionaries & Hashing
notes.subject: Data Structures
notes.content:
--- UNIT-IV SUMMARY ---

1. Dictionaries:
- Collection of key-value pairs where keys are associated with unique values.
- Representations:
  - Sorted Array: Fast search O(log n), slow insert/delete O(n).
  - Sorted Chain (Linked List): Slow search O(n), fast insert/delete O(1) once position is found.
  - Skip List: Probabilistic linked list with multiple forward pointer levels for O(log n) average search/insert.

2. Hash Table Representation:
- Maps keys to array buckets via a Hash Function H(key).
- Home bucket/bucket is the mapped array position.
- Hash Functions:
  - Division Method: H(key) = key % table_size.
  - Mid Square: Key is squared, middle digits are extracted as address.
  - Multiplicative: H(key) = floor(p * (fractional part of key * A)) with constant A.
  - Digit Folding: Key is divided into parts, which are summed together.
  - Digit Analysis: Skewed digits are deleted from key to find uniform bucket distribution.

3. Collision & Overflow Resolution:
- Collision: H(K1) == H(K2) for K1 != K2. Synonym elements map to same home bucket.
- Techniques:
  - Chaining (Separate Chaining): Linked lists are maintained at each bucket index.
  - Open Addressing:
    - Linear Probing: Linear search for next empty bucket: (H(key) + i) % m.
    - Quadratic Probing: Quadratic offset: (H(key) + i^2) % m. Solves primary clustering.
    - Double Hashing: Uses second hash function H2(key) as step offset: H_new = (H1(key) + i * H2(key)) % m.
  - Rehashing: Table size is doubled (to nearest prime) when completely full or half full (quadratic probing).

notes.title: Data Structures - UNIT 5: Binary Search Trees, AVL Trees, & B-Trees
notes.subject: Data Structures
notes.content:
--- UNIT-V SUMMARY ---

1. Trees & Traversals:
- A tree is a hierarchical structure of nodes.
- Binary Tree Traversals:
  - Pre-order: Visit Root -> Traverse Left -> Traverse Right.
  - In-order: Traverse Left -> Visit Root -> Traverse Right. (Yields sorted values in BST).
  - Post-order: Traverse Left -> Traverse Right -> Visit Root.

2. Binary Search Tree (BST) ADT:
- Binary Tree satisfying: Values in Left Subtree < Root Value < Values in Right Subtree.
- Average search/insert/delete operations are O(log n). Worst-case is O(n) for skewed trees.

3. AVL Trees:
- Height-balanced BST where Balance Factor BF(T) = h_Left - h_Right satisfies BF(T) ∈ {-1, 0, +1}.
- Violation of balance requires rebalancing rotations:
  - Single Rotations: LL (Left-Left) rotation and RR (Right-Right) rotation.
  - Double Rotations: LR (Left-Right) rotation and RL (Right-Left) rotation.
- Search, insertion, and deletion are guaranteed O(log n) time complexity.

4. B-Trees and B+ Trees:
- Multi-way search trees used for external disk storage and databases.
- B-Tree of order m definition:
  - Root has at least 2 children.
  - All internal nodes have at least ceil(m/2) children.
  - All leaf nodes are at same level.
- B+ Tree (Variation of B-Tree):
  - Data pointers are stored exclusively at the leaf level.
  - Leaf nodes are linked sequentially for quick ordered range traversal.
